metadata.title: Iran-Israel Conflict Cost Tracker
metadata.description: Comprehensive tracking of military expenditures, economic impacts, and financial costs associated with the Iran-Israel conflict escalation of 2026.

sources.name: Congressional Budget Office (CBO)
sources.description: Baseline defense spending projections and supplemental appropriations analysis

sources.name: U.S. Department of Defense (DoD)
sources.description: Official military budget data, weapon system costs, and operational expenditure reports

sources.name: Congressional Research Service (CRS)
sources.description: Non-partisan analysis of military operations costs, foreign aid, and defense procurement

sources.name: Stockholm International Peace Research Institute (SIPRI)
sources.description: International arms transfers, military expenditure databases, and conflict cost modeling

sources.name: Brown University Costs of War Project
sources.description: Comprehensive accounting of post-9/11 war costs including long-term veteran care and interest on borrowing

sources.name: Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS)
sources.description: Defense program analysis and missile defense cost assessments

metadata.methodology: Cost estimates are derived from publicly available DoD budget documents, CBO scoring of supplemental appropriations, CRS unit cost analyses for weapon systems, and SIPRI military expenditure databases. Daily operational burn rates are calculated using DoD cost-per-flying-hour data, Navy ship operating cost models, and historical precedent from Operations Inherent Resolve and Prosperity Guardian. Indirect and economic costs are modeled using IMF World Economic Outlook methodologies and historical oil price shock analyses. All figures are in 2026 USD unless otherwise noted. Estimates carry an uncertainty range of approximately +/- 15%.
metadata.disclaimer: These figures are estimates compiled from multiple open-source intelligence and government reporting channels. Actual costs may differ significantly due to classified expenditures, delayed reporting, and the inherent uncertainty of ongoing military operations. This data is presented for informational and educational purposes only. The use of named weapon systems and unit costs reflects publicly available procurement data and does not represent classified operational details.

us.name: United States

munitionsExpended.description: CSIS April 24: $24B in munitions expended in 7 weeks. 45% Precision Strike Missiles used. 50% THAAD interceptors depleted. ~50% Patriot interceptors fired (1,060-1,430 of 2,330). 1,000+ JASSM-ER. 1,000+ Tomahawks ($2M each). Restocking: 1-4 years. Pentagon FY27 $1.5T (war costs NOT included). $22B munitions procurement requested (+387%).
munitionsExpended.source: CSIS — Last Rounds: Status of Key Munitions in Iran War (April 24)

navalOperations.description: 3 carrier strike groups (Lincoln, Ford, Bush), 2,500+ Marines + USS Tripoli deployed, USS Boxer ARG + 11th MEU, destroyer/submarine operations. Day 53: CENTCOM blockade Day 8 — 27 vessels redirected, Touska seized. Sustained Hormuz patrol.
navalOperations.source: Navy Budget Office / USNI News Fleet Tracker

airOperations.description: B-2 Spirit and B-1B sorties from Whiteman AFB and Diego Garcia ($6.7M per B-2 sortie), F-35A/F-22 operations, KC-135/KC-46 tanker support (6 crew killed in KC-135 crash Day 14). 24 MQ-9 Reapers lost ($720M+). Ceasefire pauses air ops over Iran but CENTCOM maintains readiness posture.
airOperations.source: Air Force Cost Per Flying Hour data / CRS Air Force Budget Report

personnelDeployment.description: ~57,000 personnel deployed to CENTCOM AOR including 5,000 Marines (31st MEU + 2,500 additional), combat pay, hostile fire pay. 13 US service members KIA + 2 non-combat deaths. 399 WIA (354 returned to duty). No new KIA since ceasefire. CENTCOM blockade Day 8. Vance delegation en route Pakistan.

logistics.description: Strategic logistics, forward basing, fuel, maintenance, sustainment. Day 53: Elevated fuel costs from Brent $96.26 crude. CENTCOM blockade enforcement logistics — Touska tow to Fujairah. Ceasefire sustainment ops, Hormuz patrol logistics, SPR management. Includes Isfahan rescue op logistics.

directMilitaryCosts.source: DoD Comptroller / CRS estimates. Pentagon told Congress first 6 days cost $11.3B. Pentagon supplemental slashed from $200B+ to $80-100B (WaPo April 7). 250+ groups oppose additional funding. Penn Wharton: $40B-$95B total direct costs.

economicDisruption.description: Indirect economic costs including supply chain disruption, oil price impact, domestic inflation

us.dailyBurnRateNote: $800M-$1B/day current average (down from $2B/day initial surge). CSIS: $891M/day first 100h, ~$601M/day Days 7-12. Pentagon officials cite ~$1B/day. Penn Wharton: settling to ~$800M/day sustained. $200B+ supplemental pending.
us.details: CSIS: $3.7B first 100 hours, $11.3B first 6 days (Pentagon to Congress), $16.5B through Day 12. Kevin Hassett confirmed ~$12B figure as of ~Day 16. CAP projects ~$25B through Day 26. Daily costs $601M-$1B+ (settling from early $2B/day surge). Pentagon seeking $200B+ supplemental. USS Boxer + 2,500 Marines deployed (Day 21), USS Tripoli + 31st MEU arrived (Day 28), bringing total ~50,000 personnel in theater. Hegseth: '15,000+ targets struck across Iran.'
us.note: Day 57 Late: $47B direct + $37.6B indirect = ~$84.6B total. CSIS: $24B munitions in 7 weeks, 50% Patriot interceptors used. OFAC froze $344M IRGC/Hezbollah crypto. Hengli + 40 ships sanctioned. Bessent: no oil waivers. Blockade Day 13 — 34 vessels redirected. 3 carriers. Brent $105.33 (+31.9%). Gas $4.031. 13 KIA, 413 WIA.
us.totalNote: CSIS estimate through Day 12: $16.5B. CAP estimate through Day 26: ~$25B. Penn Wharton: $40B-$95B total (likely ~$65B for under 2 months). Trump admin now seeking $80-100B supplemental (down from $200B+).
us.description: US direct military costs ~$47B (Day 57). Indirect costs ~$37.6B. Total ~$84.6B. CSIS: $24B munitions in 7 weeks, 50% Patriot/THAAD depleted. OFAC: $344M crypto freeze. Hengli + 40 ships sanctioned. Bessent: no oil waivers. Blockade Day 13. Brent $105.33. Gas $4.031. 13 KIA, 413 WIA.

israel.name: Israel

munitionsExpended.description: Arrow-3 (40+ used, $2.5M each), Arrow-2 (55+ used, $3M each), Iron Dome Tamir (4,500+ used, $50K each = $225M), David's Sling (80+ used, $1M each), LORA ballistic missiles, Delilah cruise missiles, precision-guided munitions. RUSI warns Arrow stocks near total depletion. $1.25B Raytheon-Rafael contract to surge Tamir production. Finance Ministry: $17.5B total through 40 days.
munitionsExpended.source: Israeli Ministry of Defense / SIPRI Arms Transfers Database

navalOperations.description: Sa'ar 6 corvette operations, submarine deployments in Eastern Mediterranean, naval blockade support, maritime patrol operations. Ashdod port defense after Hezbollah deep strike.

airOperations.description: F-35I, F-15I, F-16 sorties over Iran and Lebanon. A-10 and Apache deployments. IAF operations now entering Week 7. 5 IDF divisions in Lebanon (98th, 36th, 91st, 146th, 162nd). Operation Eternal Darkness air campaign.
airOperations.source: Israeli Air Force / CSIS Missile Defense Project

personnelDeployment.description: Full IDF reserve mobilization of ~300,000 reservists, combat pay, civil defense activation, Home Front Command operations. 5 divisions deployed to Lebanon ground ops (Operation Roaring Lion).
personnelDeployment.source: Israeli Ministry of Finance / Bank of Israel

logistics.description: Emergency resupply from US stockpiles (WRSA-I), domestic ammunition production surge, civil defense shelter operations, critical infrastructure hardening, Lebanon ground ops sustainment.

directMilitaryCosts.source: Israel Finance Ministry: $17.5B for 40 days of war on Iran + Lebanon (April 9). | Knesset: record $45.8B defense budget + $13B supplemental approved. | Middle East Monitor April 9. | Breaking Defense.

economicDisruption.description: GDP loss from 300,000 reserve mobilization, Ben Gurion Airport closure, tourism collapse. Bloomberg: $13B defense supplement approved, $2.9B emergency supplement. Total 2026 defense budget $34.63B (112B shekels).

oilPriceImpact.description: Israel's energy import cost surge from elevated global oil and natural gas prices, emergency fuel purchases, and Tamar/Leviathan gas platform security operations

tradeDisruption.description: Disruption to high-tech exports, closure of Haifa and Ashdod ports to commercial traffic during threat windows, canceled international business contracts, and foreign investment flight
tradeDisruption.source: Israel Export Institute / Central Bureau of Statistics

humanitarian.description: Civil defense operations, emergency medical services surge, psychological trauma support, damaged infrastructure repair, and civilian evacuation programs from northern and southern border communities

israel.dailyBurnRateNote: Calcalist estimates $480-550M/day average ($645-970M/day first 3 days). Includes missile defense operations, IAF sorties (60 jets dropped 150+ bombs Day 28 alone), 300,000 reserve mobilization, and civil defense.
israel.note: Day 43: Finance Ministry estimates $17.5B for 40 days of war on Iran + Lebanon. Daily spending ~$400M/day. Bank of Israel total economic toll across all fronts: $112B. Knesset approved $45.8B defense budget for 2026.
notes: Knesset approved record NIS 143B ($45.8B) 2026 defense budget on March 30 — $9.48B increase over 2025. Includes NIS 30B ($9.5B) wartime funding. Total state budget NIS 850.6B ($271B), largest in Israeli history. New $48M deal with Elbit for 155mm shells.
israel.description: Israel direct military costs ~$17.5B (Day 43). Finance Ministry: $17.5B for 40 days of war. Total defense budget $45.8B. $400-500M/day average. Knesset approved record budget March 30. 5 IDF divisions in Lebanon.

iran.name: Iran

munitionsExpended.description: Missiles, rockets, drones expended in 8+ salvo attacks on Israel and Gulf states. Ballistic missile stockpile significantly reduced per IDF intelligence.

navalOperations.description: IRGC Navy fast attack craft, mine-laying in Hormuz approaches. 50+ naval vessels sunk/damaged, 90+ total. Navy effectively destroyed per Hegseth.

airOperations.description: IRIAF defensive sorties (limited due to coalition air superiority), drone swarm operations, air defense network activation, and dispersal of remaining air assets

personnelDeployment.description: Full mobilization of IRGC ground forces, Basij militia activation of approximately 600,000 personnel, Artesh regular army deployment to border regions, and civil defense operations

logistics.description: Dispersal of military assets to hardened facilities, tunnel network operations, emergency fuel and ammunition distribution, field hospital establishment, and command-and-control reconstitution

economicDisruption.description: Destruction of critical infrastructure including Bandar Abbas port facilities, Isfahan industrial complex damage, electrical grid degradation, and telecommunications network disruption

oilPriceImpact.description: Near-total loss of oil export revenue. Kharg Island military targets destroyed (oil infrastructure spared but threatened). Chatham House projects >10% GDP decline. Iran internet at ~4% of normal for 15 days.
oilPriceImpact.source: EIA / OPEC Monthly Oil Market Report

tradeDisruption.description: Collapse of non-oil trade through damaged ports, land border crossing disruptions, sanctions tightening cutting off remaining trade partners, and rial currency collapse

humanitarian.description: Mass displacement of approximately 2.1 million civilians from strike zones, hospital system strain from casualties, food and medicine distribution disruption, and water treatment facility damage

iran.dailyBurnRateNote: Estimated daily cost including military operations, infrastructure losses, and oil revenue foregone

weaponCosts.description: Land-attack cruise missile launched from Arleigh Burke-class destroyers and Virginia-class submarines against Iranian military and nuclear facilities. 850+ fired through Day 29 — approximately 9× annual procurement rate. Pentagon approaching 'Winchester' status for Tomahawks in CENTCOM.
weaponCosts.source: CRS Report: Navy Tomahawk Cruise Missile Program

weaponCosts.description: 30,000-lb bunker-buster bomb delivered by B-2 Spirit stealth bombers against deeply buried Iranian nuclear enrichment facilities at Fordow and Natanz
weaponCosts.source: DoD Selected Acquisition Report / Air Force Magazine

weaponCosts.description: Extended-range joint air-to-surface standoff missile launched from B-1B Lancers, F-15E Strike Eagles, and F-35A Lightning IIs against IRGC command centers and air defense sites
weaponCosts.source: CRS Report: Joint Air-to-Surface Standoff Missile

weaponCosts.description: Aegis Ballistic Missile Defense interceptor launched from destroyers to intercept Iranian medium-range ballistic missiles targeting US bases and allied positions
weaponCosts.source: CRS Report: Navy Aegis BMD Program / MDA Budget

weaponCosts.description: Extended range active missile used for anti-air warfare and terminal ballistic missile defense against Iranian cruise missile and drone salvos
weaponCosts.source: CRS Report: Navy SM-6 Missile Program

weaponCosts.description: Precision-guided 250-lb bombs delivered by F-35A and F-15E aircraft against dispersed IRGC ground targets, radar installations, and mobile missile launchers

Short-range interceptor missiles used to defend Israeli population centers against Iranian rocket, drone, and (unexpectedly) some ballistic missile attacks. Iron Dome now performing ballistic intercepts as Arrow stocks deplete. $1.25B Raytheon-Rafael contract to surge Tamir production.
weaponCosts.source: CSIS Missile Defense Project / Rafael Advanced Defense Systems

weaponCosts.description: Exo-atmospheric ballistic missile defense interceptor used against Iranian Emad and Sejjil medium-range ballistic missiles; intercepts targets in space before reentry. RUSI warns Arrow-3 stocks 'near total depletion' after 29 days of sustained mass intercept operations. Could be fully exhausted by end of March.
weaponCosts.source: CSIS Missile Defense Project / Israel Missile Defense Organization

weaponCosts.description: Endo-atmospheric ballistic missile defense interceptor providing lower-tier defense against Iranian ballistic missiles that penetrate Arrow-3 engagement envelope. More than half of pre-war inventory depleted in first 96 hours. UK declined US request to share interceptors.

Medium-range interceptor bridging the gap between Iron Dome and Arrow systems; used against Iranian cruise missiles and large-caliber rockets. Heavily tasked as Arrow stocks depleted. ~$700K-$1M per interceptor.

Delta-wing loitering munition/kamikaze drone with ~2,500km range and 50kg warhead; launched in swarms to overwhelm Israeli air defenses and strike infrastructure. Iran averaging ~94 drones/day after initial phase. Drones down 95% per Hegseth.
Solid-fueled tactical ballistic missile with ~300km range; launched from mobile TELs against US military positions in Iraq, Bahrain, and Qatar
Maneuverable reentry vehicle ballistic missile with ~1,700km range; primary weapon used in strikes against Israeli territory from western Iran launch sites
Two-stage solid-fueled MRBM with ~2,000km range; Iran's most capable ballistic missile with reduced launch preparation time and higher speed at reentry
Ground-launched cruise missile with ~1,650km range; used alongside ballistic missiles to create complex attack profiles against Israeli and US targets
Chinese-derived anti-ship missile deployed by IRGC Navy coastal batteries and fast attack craft against coalition naval vessels in Strait of Hormuz approaches

navalDeployments.description: US Navy force posture deployed to CENTCOM AOR and Eastern Mediterranean in response to Iran-Israel conflict
navalDeployments.carrierGroupDailyCostNote: Estimated daily operating cost per carrier strike group including air wing operations, escort vessels, and logistics ships based on Navy Visibility and Management of Operating and Support Costs (VAMOSC)
navalDeployments.destroyerDailyCostNote: Estimated daily operating cost per Arleigh Burke-class guided-missile destroyer at wartime tempo
navalDeployments.estimatedDailyCostNote: Combined daily cost of 3 carrier strike groups + USS Tripoli amphibious group + support vessels. France Charles de Gaulle + 10 warships not included.
navalDeployments.totalNavalCostNote: Total naval deployment cost for 29 days of active conflict operations ($25M/day × 29 days)
navalDeployments.source: Navy VAMOSC / CRS Naval Operations Budget Reports

aidPackages.name: FY2024 National Security Supplemental - Israel Component
aidPackages.description: Part of the $95 billion national security supplemental package signed into law. Includes $4 billion for Iron Dome and David's Sling replenishment, $1.2 billion for Iron Beam development, $4.4 billion for current military operations, and $9.1 billion in humanitarian aid for Gaza. Also includes $3.5 billion for advanced weapons procurement and $4.2 billion for defense articles and services.

aidPackages.name: US-Israel 10-Year MOU Annual Allocation (FY2026)
aidPackages.description: Annual military aid under the 2016 US-Israel Memorandum of Understanding signed by the Obama administration. Provides $3.3 billion in Foreign Military Financing (FMF) and $500 million in missile defense cooperation. MOU runs from FY2019 through FY2028 at $38 billion total over 10 years.
aidPackages.source: CRS Report: US Foreign Aid to Israel

aidPackages.name: Emergency Iron Dome Replenishment (FY2025)
aidPackages.description: Emergency appropriation for Iron Dome Tamir interceptor replenishment following the June 2025 Twelve-Day War. Funds accelerated production of approximately 20,000 additional Tamir interceptors and spare components for Iron Dome batteries.

aidPackages.name: FY2026 Emergency Supplemental for Israel (Pending)
aidPackages.description: Emergency supplemental appropriations request submitted to Congress on March 4, 2026. Expected to include Iron Dome and Arrow interceptor replenishment, precision-guided munitions resupply from WRSA-I drawdown, F-35I replacement parts, and humanitarian assistance. CBO preliminary score pending. Estimates range from $12-18 billion based on historical precedent and current operational tempo.

globalGDPImpact.description: Estimated reduction in global GDP growth for 2026 due to oil price shock, trade disruption, and financial market volatility caused by the conflict and Strait of Hormuz blockage
globalGDPImpact.source: IMF World Economic Outlook methodology / historical oil shock modeling

oilPriceShock.estimatedGlobalCostNote: Annualized additional cost to global economy if prices remain elevated at Brent $112.57, based on approximately 100 million barrels/day global consumption. Macquarie warns 40% probability of $200 oil if Hormuz stays shut through June.

hormuzFlowDisruption.description: Approximately 21 million barrels per day normally transit the Strait of Hormuz (~21% of global oil consumption). IRGC enforcing effective blockade — only 21 tankers have transited since war began vs 100+/day pre-war. Iran operates 'toll booth' system allowing select friendly nations (China, Russia, India, Pakistan, Iraq, Thailand, Malaysia). Overall tanker traffic down 95%+. Only ~0.5 MBD trickling through via tolled passages.

tradeDisruption.globalTradeImpactUnit: percentage reduction in global trade volume
tradeDisruption.affectedTradeVolumeNote: Annual value of trade normally transiting the Strait of Hormuz beyond oil (LNG, petrochemicals, containerized goods)
tradeDisruption.description: Major disruption to global trade routes through Persian Gulf. LNG shipments from Qatar severely impacted. Container shipping rerouted around Cape of Good Hope. Middle East-Asia trade corridors experiencing 2-3 week delays.
tradeDisruption.source: World Trade Organization / UNCTAD Review of Maritime Transport

shippingInsuranceSurge.affectedArea: Persian Gulf, Gulf of Oman, Strait of Hormuz, Arabian Sea (north of 20°N)
shippingInsuranceSurge.description: War risk insurance premiums for vessels transiting the Persian Gulf have surged from 0.05% to 5% of hull value. A typical VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier) valued at $120 million now faces $6 million in war risk premiums per transit compared to $60,000 pre-conflict. Many underwriters have suspended coverage entirely.
shippingInsuranceSurge.source: Lloyd's Market Association Joint War Committee

economicImpact.stockMarketImpact: S&P 500 -1.67% Day 28 (7-month low, 5th straight weekly loss). Dow -1.73% officially in correction. Nasdaq underperforming. Fed holds rates at 3.5-3.75%, warns inflation progress stalled. Hedge funds worst drawdowns since Liberation Day (JPMorgan). Macquarie: 'crude will trade like a meme stock until the solution is peace.'
economicImpact.oilPriceImpact: Brent $106.42/bbl (+33.3% from pre-war $79.84). WTI $104.21 (+38.0% from $75.51). Reversed earlier dip to $101 after Trump prime-time Oval Office address vowing to hit Iran 'extremely hard.' April 6 Hormuz energy deadline. IEA: largest supply disruption in history — 21 MBD to ~0.5 MBD through Hormuz. Goldman warns $147+. Macquarie: 40% chance of $200 if Hormuz shut through June.
economicImpact.sanctionsNote: Trump temporarily lifted sanctions on ~140M barrels Iranian crude at sea (March 20). Jones Act and Russian oil sanctions also temporarily suspended to ease energy prices.

keyFacts.fact: The US has expended approximately 400 Tomahawk cruise missiles in the first week of operations, representing roughly 25% of the Navy's estimated active inventory and requiring 2-3 years of production to replace at current manufacturing rates.
keyFacts.source: CRS Report: Navy Tomahawk Cruise Missile Program

keyFacts.fact: Israel's multi-layered missile defense system (Iron Dome, David's Sling, Arrow-2, Arrow-3) has intercepted over 85% of incoming threats, but at a combined cost exceeding $242 million in interceptor missiles alone during the first 7 days.

Each B-2 Spirit stealth bomber sortie from Whiteman AFB, Missouri to Iranian targets and back costs approximately $6.7 million in fuel, maintenance, and munitions, not including the GBU-57 MOPs which add $3.5 million each.
keyFacts.source: Air Force Cost Per Flying Hour Reports / GAO

keyFacts.fact: The Strait of Hormuz blockage is costing the global economy an estimated $3.5 billion per day in disrupted oil flows, elevated energy prices, rerouted shipping, and cascading supply chain impacts across Asia and Europe.

Iran's 500+ Shahed-136 drones cost approximately $10 million total to produce -- less than the cost of a single SM-3 interceptor ($12M) used to shoot down a ballistic missile, highlighting the asymmetric cost advantage of drone warfare.

The combined US military aid to Israel since FY2024, including the 10-year MOU, supplemental packages, and emergency appropriations, now exceeds $31 billion -- approaching the annual GDP of Jordan ($33B) and more than the entire GDP of Iceland ($28B).
keyFacts.source: CRS Report: US Foreign Aid to Israel

keyFacts.fact: Biggest weekly gain in crude oil futures history -- WTI up 35.1% and Brent up 41.6% from pre-war levels, surpassing the 1990 Gulf War spike and 2022 Russia-Ukraine oil shock.
VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier) charter rates surged from $50,000/day to $400,000/day -- an 8x increase -- as vessel supply collapsed due to the Hormuz blockade and insurance withdrawal.

CSIS estimates the war cost the US $16.5 billion through Day 12 — an average of $1.375 billion per day. The Pentagon told senators the first 6 days alone cost $11.3 billion, with $5.6 billion spent on munitions in just the first 48 hours.

10,000 AI-powered Merops interceptor drones were deployed to the Middle East in under 5 days — each costs $14,000-$15,000 compared to $4M+ for a Patriot interceptor. The entire deployment cost roughly $150M, replacing billions in traditional air defense expenditure.
Penn Wharton estimates $40B-$95B in direct costs, with up to $210B in broader economic impact from the Iran-Israel conflict.
A 60-day war would increase the US deficit by $66.4 billion according to Fortune analysis, factoring in military spending, economic disruption, and reduced tax revenue.
F1 cancels Bahrain and Saudi Arabia GPs — estimated $100M combined hosting fee loss
Maritime insurance premiums up 1,000%+; container surcharges $1,500-2,000/TEU
Italian MQ-9A Reaper destroyed at Ali Al Salem, Kuwait — $30-35M asset loss
Gold above $5,000/oz; Iranian rial crashed to 1.75M/USD (all-time low, down 30% YTD)
Trump adviser: US direct war cost $12B so far; Pentagon briefed $11.3B first 6 days + $1B/day ongoing
Israel approved NIS 2.6B ($826M) emergency defense procurement
Dubai airport drone strike — 7+ hours flight suspension, estimated tens of millions in losses
Fujairah oil hub struck 2nd consecutive day — port operations repeatedly disrupted

keyFacts.text: White House economic adviser Kevin Hassett confirmed $12B US spending through Day 16. Pentagon supplemental request of $50B being drafted.
Israel Knesset voting to raise deficit ceiling from 3.9% to 5.1% GDP — NIS 32B ($10.2B) additional spending. Updated defense budget: NIS 143B. Iran war has cost Israel NIS 9B so far.
Bank of America warns markets 'underpricing risk,' conflict extending to Q2 'equally likely.' Raised Brent forecast to potential $130/bbl if disruptions persist into late 2026.

Pentagon told senators first 6 days cost $11.3B. 250+ groups (ACLU, Public Citizen) oppose additional war funding. $50B+ supplemental being drafted. Jeffries won't commit to blocking funding. Daily costs $890M-$2B.

Hedge funds suffering worst drawdowns since Liberation Day amid Iran war fallout (JPMorgan). Fed holds rates at 3.5-3.75%; inflation forecast raised to ~3% through late 2026. Fed may cut rate-cut forecast from 1 to zero.

Pentagon requests $200B+ war supplemental from Congress — far exceeding the $50B initially drafted. Would urgently increase production of critical munitions. Dow drops 768 pts (-1.63%) after Fed holds rates, warns inflation 'not coming down as hoped.'

Fed holds rates at 3.5-3.75%, projects only 1 rate cut in 2026. Raises inflation outlook to 2.7%. Powell says oil crisis may have 'only temporary effects' but admits progress on inflation has stalled. S&P 500 -1.36%, Nasdaq -1.46%.

Pentagon requests $200B+ war supplemental — far exceeding initial $50B draft. Hegseth: 'largest strike package yet' coming. Dubai crude hits record $166.80. European jet fuel at $220/barrel. QatarEnergy declares force majeure; 17% LNG capacity offline 3-5 years.

keyFacts.fact: Pentagon requests $200B+ supplemental war budget. CSIS: $16.5B in first 12 days. Federal debt surpassed $39T.

Trump administration temporarily lifted sanctions on Iranian crude oil stranded at sea (March 20) — allowing US allies to purchase ~140M barrels. Jones Act temporarily suspended to ease domestic fuel transport. Russian oil sanctions temporarily lifted. Treasury Secretary Bessent called measures 'narrowly tailored.'
Macquarie forecasts 40% probability of oil reaching $200/barrel if Hormuz stays shut through June 2026; Brent closed Day 28 at $112.57/barrel — highest since 2022; WTI briefly crossed $100

IMF March 30: Iran war is 'largest disruption to global oil market in history.' Every 10% oil price rise = +0.4% global inflation and -0.2% GDP. OECD: US inflation forecast raised to 4.2% (+1.2pp). Goldman Sachs: 30% US recession probability over next 12 months. G20 inflation projected at ~4% for 2026.

Bank of Israel held interest rates at 4% on March 30, citing war inflation. GDP forecast: 3.8% in 2026, 5.5% in 2027. Inflation forecast: 2.2% in 2026. Shekel weakened 0.6% to 3.1675/USD near 2026 low. Israel war costs ~$3B/week under current restrictions.

Knesset approved record $45.8B defense budget (62-55 vote, March 29-30), including NIS 30B+ for Operation Roaring Lion. Total 2026 budget ~$271B. Ultra-Orthodox parties received $750M+ additional funds. Prevents snap elections Netanyahu would lose.
Trump requests record $1.5T defense budget ($1.15T base + $350B reconciliation) — 44% increase. Includes $50B missile/interceptor replacement supplemental.
Israel Knesset approved record $45.8B defense budget (March 30), including NIS 30B+ for Operation Roaring Lion.

Pentagon FY2027 budget: $1.5T total. Drone spending TRIPLED to $74B. Tomahawk orders 14x (55→785). $55B for new Defense Autonomous Warfare Group (DAWG) — 24,000% YoY. $30B+ munitions. 44,500 new troops (+2%). Largest shipbuilding request since 1962. Iran war drives biggest defense restructuring in decades.

Harvard Kennedy School professor Linda Bilmes: 'certain' the war will cost $1 trillion over the next decade. Pentagon first 6 days: $11.3B. Daily cost estimate: ~$2B/day. US debt context: $31T in public debt, 15% of budget on interest (vs 7% during Iraq/Afghanistan).
Treasury sanctions 35 entities in Iran 'shadow banking architecture' under Operation Economic Fury, targeting tens of billions in illicit funding.

Pentagon CFO testifies: Iran war has cost $25 billion through 60 days (first official figure). Mostly munitions. 45% PrSM, ~50% THAAD/Patriot interceptors expended. Pentagon seeking $200B supplemental. Harvard Kennedy School: 'certain' to cost $1 trillion over next decade.
Fed holds rates at 3.5-3.75% with rare 8-4 vote (most dissent since October 1992). Iran rial hits record low 1.81M/USD, down 12% in one week. IMF: Iran GDP -6.1%, inflation 68.9%.
Rubio invokes emergency to bypass Congress on $8.6B arms sales: Qatar $4.01B Patriot, Kuwait $2.5B battle command, Israel $992M APKWS, UAE APKWS. Third congressional bypass of conflict.

Treasury sanctions Hengli Petrochemical (Dalian) + ~40 shipping firms + 19 shadow fleet vessels + 35 shadow banking entities. State Dept sanctions Qingdao Haiye Oil Terminal. Blockade cost Iran $4.8B — 31 tankers/53M barrels stuck.
Spirit Airlines ceases operations — first US corporate casualty of the Iran war. Jet fuel doubled from $2.24 to $4.51/gallon. Failed to secure $500M federal bailout.

totals.description: Combined direct military + indirect economic costs through Day 65 PM PT. Pentagon official: $25B US direct military (CNN: true cost $40-50B). IRGC warns war 'likely' to resume. $8.6B arms bypass Congress. IDF destroys 120 Hezbollah sites; 41 killed in Lebanon. Trump 'hostilities terminated' letter. Iran 14-point proposal rejected. Treasury sanctions Hengli + 40 ships. Blockade cost Iran $4.8B. Spirit Airlines folds. Brent $111.25 (+39.3%), WTI $103.82 (+37.5%). AAA gas $4.433. Rial 1,776,000/USD. Hormuz Day 62.

totalFormatted: $77.92B (direct military, all countries, Day 65 PM PT — Pentagon official: US $25B direct, true cost $40-50B. $8.6B arms bypass Congress. IRGC warns war resumption 'likely.' Blockade cost Iran $4.8B. Spirit Airlines first US corporate casualty. Israel NIS 35B ($11.5B). Iran $270B+ infrastructure/economic. Treasury sanctions Hengli + 40 ships. 50% of key munitions depleted. FY2027 defense request $1.5T (+42%). Rial 1.776M/USD.)
